claim.content: Comparing the fitted Fermi-liquid transport coefficient $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ with the measured Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ yields an $A/\gamma^{2}$ value that lies near the empirical Kadowaki–Woods scaling band $A/\gamma^{2}\sim 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}\ (\mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}})^{-2}$ often observed for heavy-fermion materials, and therefore the transport and heat-capacity data of this compound are consistent with enhanced effective mass and strong electronic correlations within the usual empirical uncertainty of that relation.

conclusion.content: Comparing the fitted Fermi-liquid transport coefficient $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ with the measured Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ yields an $A/\gamma^{2}$ value that lies near the empirical Kadowaki–Woods scaling band $A/\gamma^{2}\sim 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}\ (\mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}})^{-2}$ often observed for heavy-fermion materials, and therefore the transport and heat-capacity data of this compound are consistent with enhanced effective mass and strong electronic correlations within the usual empirical uncertainty of that relation.

premises.content: The empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation, in which the ratio $A/\gamma^{2}$ for many heavy-fermion compounds clusters near $10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}\ (\mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}})^{-2}$, is applied to the measured parameters $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ and $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ of CeAg$_{0.68}$Si$_{1.32}$ to assess heavy-fermion character; this empirical comparison is indicative but not definitive because $A/\gamma^{2}$ shows known material-specific scatter and depends on details such as carrier concentration and dominant scattering channels that are not constrained here.

steps.reasoning:
Upstream results used as starting points: the low-temperature resistivity analysis yielded $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ (from the $\rho=\rho_{0}+A T^{2}$ fit) and the heat-capacity analysis yielded $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ/mol\ K^{2}}$; these two experimentally determined parameters are taken as known inputs for the Kadowaki–Woods comparison.
(Upstream conclusions referenced: conclusion id="8" and conclusion id="7")
Fig. 10; Fig. 9

Statement of the Kadowaki–Woods empirical relation: the Kadowaki–Woods relation for heavy-fermion systems is given in the paper as $A/\gamma^{2}\sim 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}\ (\mathrm{mJ\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-2}})^{-2}$, an empirical scaling known to hold approximately for many heavy-fermion compounds and cited by the authors.
[52]

Comparison and conclusion: using the experimentally measured $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ and $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ/mol\ K^{2}}$, the authors state that the compound is consistent with the Kadowaki–Woods empirical scaling (i.e., $A/\gamma^{2}$ is of the same order as $10^{-5}$ in the units given), and they take this consistency as further support for enhanced effective mass and strong electronic correlations characteristic of heavy-fermion systems.
Fig. 10; Fig. 9

motivating_questions.content: A standard empirical test for the consistency of enhanced $\gamma$ (thermodynamic mass) with transport scattering is the Kadowaki–Woods relation. Before this study, no simultaneous values of $A$ and $\gamma$ were reported for CeAg$_{0.68}$Si$_{1.32}$, so it was unknown whether transport and thermodynamic indicators of correlations followed the heavy-fermion scaling. Demonstrating that $A/\gamma^{2}$ lies near the canonical heavy-fermion value supports the interpretation that mass enhancement is intrinsic and reflected across thermodynamic and transport observables.
